key_insight: A physics-informed residual neural network can model hot deformation and dynamic recrystallization in a Mo-rich titanium alloy more accurately and more physically than standard constitutive equations.
engineering_relevance: This enables more reliable process design for hot working, forging, and thermomechanical scheduling of Ti-6Al-4Mo-1V-0.1Si components.
method: Built two stacked residual physics-informed neural networks (STAR-PINNs) from flow-stress data across 800–1050 °C and 0.01–10 s⁻¹.
Enforced constitutive physics such as strain hardening, strain-rate sensitivity, thermal softening, post-peak softening, and DRV/DRX behavior via automatic differentiation.
Added a DRX-aware dual-output design with JMAK-Avrami regularization, DRX saturation constraints, and Arrhenius consistency to link recrystallization fraction with stress.
evidence_signal: The DRX-aware model achieved RMSE 11.69 MPa, MAE 4.83 MPa, R² 0.9850, and cross-validated RMSE 12.47 ± 0.26 MPa while reproducing flow curves, DRX kinetics, and Zener-Hollomon trends.
watch_item: Performance is demonstrated on one alloy dataset, so transferability to other alloys, wider strain paths, or processing windows still needs validation.
paper_title: Physics-Informed Residual Deep Learning for Constitutive Modeling of Hot Deformation and Dynamic Recrystallization in a Mo-Rich $α+β$ Titanium Alloy
